name: Deep Dive into the Java List Interface
chatResponse: I'll break down the key characteristics of the List interface and compare its two most common implementations: ArrayList and LinkedList.

steps.title: Core Characteristics
steps.content: A `List` is an ordered collection, often called a **sequence**. Unlike a `Set`, a `List` allows for duplicate elements. Its defining feature is **positional access**: you can insert or retrieve elements based on their numerical index in the list, starting from 0. It also provides a special iterator called a `ListIterator` that allows for bidirectional traversal (forward and backward).

steps.title: ArrayList: The Dynamic Array
steps.content:
The `ArrayList` is the most commonly used implementation. Internally, it uses a standard array that grows dynamically as you add elements. 
- **Pros**: extremely fast random access (getting an element at index $i$ is $O(1)$).
- **Cons**: adding or removing elements from the middle is slow ($O(n)$) because all subsequent elements must be shifted in memory.

steps.title: LinkedList: The Linked Chain
steps.content:
A `LinkedList` stores its elements in 'nodes.' Each node contains the data and a pointer to both the next and previous nodes in the sequence. 
- **Pros**: adding or removing elements from the head or tail is very fast ($O(1)$).
- **Cons**: accessing an element by index is slow ($O(n)$) because the JVM must start at the beginning (or end) and follow the pointers until it reaches the target index.

steps.title: Implementation Comparison
steps.content:
| Operation | ArrayList | LinkedList |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **get(index)** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **add(element)** | $O(1)$ (amortized) | $O(1)$ |
| **remove(index)** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Iterator.remove()** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Memory** | Less (compact array) | More (storing node pointers) |
